question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send ?nonce=2&nonce=1 to /api/v1/transactions/:id, causing COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS collapse to select nonce=1 (last value) instead of nonce=2, thereby silently redirecting the transaction lookup to a different child transaction and reorganizing which transaction record is returned to all observers?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send ?scheduled=false&scheduled=true to /api/v1/transactions/:id, exploiting COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS collapse so that scheduled=true (last value) is used, causing the scheduled version of a transaction to be returned instead of the parent, effectively reorganizing which transaction appears in the history for that ID?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send ?scheduled=true&scheduled=false to /api/v1/transactions/:id, exploiting COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS collapse so that scheduled=false (last value) is used, causing the unscheduled parent transaction to be returned instead of the scheduled child, reorganizing the visible transaction history for that ID?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send ?nonce=1&nonce=2&nonce=3 to /api/v1/transactions/:id, exploiting COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS collapse to always select the last nonce value, allowing the attacker to deterministically control which nonce-indexed child transaction is returned, reorganizing the visible transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send multiple block.hash values (e.g., ?block.hash=0xAAA&block.hash=0xBBB) to a contracts/results endpoint, exploiting COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS collapse to silently discard all but the last block.hash, causing the query to return results from a different block than the user specified, reorganizing which contract execution history is visible?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request to /api/v1/transactions with ?timestamp=lt:T2&timestamp=T1 (operator-prefixed first), exploiting the documented order-sensitivity of NON_SORTED_PARAMS where timestamp order is preserved, to receive a 200 response with transactions, whereas the reversed order ?timestamp=T1&timestamp=lt:T2 returns a 404, effectively making transactions appear or disappear based on parameter ordering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request to /api/v1/tokens/:id with ?timestamp=lt:1234567899.999999000&timestamp=1234567899.999999001 (as documented in the NON_SORTED_PARAMS comment), exploiting preserved timestamp ordering to get a 200 response, while the reversed order returns a 404, allowing the attacker to selectively hide or reveal token history records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send ?block.number=11&block.number=10 to /api/v1/contracts/results, exploiting the documented order-sensitivity of NON_SORTED_PARAMS where block.number order is preserved, to receive different contract result ordering than ?block.number=10&block.number=11, effectively reorganizing the visible contract execution history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send ?block.number=10&block.number=11 to /api/v1/contracts/results, exploiting the documented order-sensitivity of NON_SORTED_PARAMS, to receive a different set of contract results than ?block.number=11&block.number=10, causing the transaction history to appear reorganized depending on parameter order?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lexicographic sort applied to non-NON_SORTED_PARAMS (e.g., account.id) by sending values like ?account.id=gt:9&account.id=lt:10, where lexicographic sort produces a different order than numeric sort (gt:9 > lt:10 lexicographically), causing the SQL query to apply filters in an unintended order and reorganize which transactions are returned?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user omit the 'order' parameter from a /api/v1/transactions request, causing normalizeRequestQueryParams() to inject the OpenAPI default value (e.g., 'desc'), which changes the sort order of the transaction history from what the user intended, effectively reorganizing the visible transaction sequence?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user omit the 'limit' parameter from a /api/v1/transactions request, causing normalizeRequestQueryParams() to inject the OpenAPI default value (e.g., 25), which combined with a specific 'order' default creates a normalized cache key that serves a different page of transaction history than the user's original intent?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to an unknown/undocumented path (not in openApiMap), causing normalizeRequestQueryParams() to fall back to querystring.stringify(query) which reorders parameters differently than the original request, potentially changing the SQL query behavior and reorganizing which transactions are returned?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: appendToQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a value containing '&' or '=' characters into a query parameter (e.g., ?account.id=gt:0.0.1%26timestamp=lt:9999), exploiting the raw string concatenation in appendToQuery() to inject additional query parameters into the normalized URL, reorganizing the transaction history query?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send a large number of timestamp values (e.g., ?timestamp=T1&timestamp=T2&...&timestamp=TN) to /api/v1/transactions, exploiting the fact that timestamp is in NON_SORTED_PARAMS (order preserved, not collapsed), to craft a specific ordering that causes the SQL query to return a different set of transactions than expected, reorganizing the visible history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send ?timestamp=gte:T1&timestamp=lte:T2 vs ?timestamp=lte:T2&timestamp=gte:T1 to /api/v1/transactions, exploiting the order-sensitivity of NON_SORTED_PARAMS to receive different transaction sets, effectively reorganizing which transactions are visible in the history window?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request where the normalized URL (produced by normalizeRequestQueryParams()) matches the cache key of a different user's cached response, causing the cache to serve the wrong transaction history page to the requesting user, reorganizing what transaction data they see?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send ?balance=true&balance=false to /api/v1/accounts, exploiting COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS collapse (last value wins: balance=false), causing account balance information to be excluded from the response, which reorganizes the visible account/transaction history by hiding balance data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send ?balance=false&balance=true to /api/v1/accounts, exploiting COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS collapse (last value wins: balance=true), causing account balance information to be included when the user intended to exclude it, reorganizing the visible account data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send repeated nonce values in reverse order (e.g., ?nonce=3&nonce=2&nonce=1) to /api/v1/transactions/:id, exploiting COLLAPSABLE_PARAMS collapse to always select nonce=1 (last value), causing the first child transaction to always be returned regardless of the attacker's stated intent, reorganizing which transaction record is visible?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that normalizeRequestQueryParams() rebuilds the query string in OpenAPI parameter definition order (not user-supplied order), causing sortable parameters like account.id to be reordered relative to timestamp parameters, which changes the SQL query plan and reorganizes which transactions are returned?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send ?timestamp=gt:T1&timestamp=lt:T2&timestamp=gt:T3 to /api/v1/transactions, exploiting the preserved order of NON_SORTED_PARAMS to create an ambiguous timestamp range that causes the SQL query to return a different set of transactions than a logically equivalent but differently ordered request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: normalizeRequestQueryParams()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with an openApiRoute that maps to an empty parameter list in openApiMap, causing normalizeRequestQueryParams() to fall back to querystring.stringify(query) which may encode parameters differently (e.g., array notation), reorganizing the transaction history query behavior?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send multiple limit values (e.g., ?limit=2&limit=3&limit=5) to /api/v1/accounts, exploiting the fact that limit is not in NON_SORTED_PARAMS (so it gets sorted lexicographically: 2,3,5) and all values are preserved, causing the backend to use an unexpected limit value and reorganize how many transactions are returned per page?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestNormalizer.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getNormalizedArrayValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send multiple order values (e.g., ?order=desc&order=asc) to /api/v1/transactions, exploiting the lexicographic sort of non-NON_SORTED_PARAMS (asc < desc, so result is order=asc&order=desc), causing the backend to use an unexpected sort order and reorganize the transaction history sequence?
